steps.title: Identify what the claim requires
steps.content: Bura and colleagues claim that caterpillar sounds are directed primarily at predators, not at members of the same species. A supporting finding must show that sounds are triggered by predator threat and absent otherwise — that is, a stimulus-response pattern tied to attack, not to intraspecies communication.

steps.title: Test each choice against the claim
steps.content:
• Choice A — None of the species that emitted sounds did so before the simulated attacks, and nearly all stopped emitting sounds within ten seconds after the attacks.: Sounds appeared only during attacks and ceased almost immediately after — a classic stimulus-response pattern diagnostic of predator defense, not ongoing communication. ✓
  • Choice B — In most cases, the sound that a caterpillar species produced during simulated attacks was not produced by other caterpillar species during simulated attacks.: Describes variation in sound type across species. This says nothing about whether sounds are directed at predators versus conspecifics. ✗
  • Choice C — Caterpillar clicks were emitted in a frequency detectable by birds that prey on caterpillars, but caterpillar vocalizations were not.: Shows one sound type is audible to predators, but the other is not — this actually weakens the claim that all recorded sounds target predators. ✗
  • Choice D — Each caterpillar species tended to produce one sound during simulated attacks, although individuals occasionally made a variety of other sounds during simulated attacks as well.: Describes consistency of sound within a species during attacks, not whether sounds are predator-directed rather than conspecific communication. ✗
That matches Choice A — None of the species that emitted sounds did so before the simulated attacks, and nearly all stopped emitting sounds within ten seconds after the attacks..
